Hashing all values removes their interpretability and can harm model performance without ensuring true privacy. Dimensionality reduction like PCA can hide feature meaning but still carry sensitive information in the new representation. Removing all four fields eliminates potentially valuable predictors and can weaken the model unnecessarily.

question:
A media analytics company is building a TensorFlow Extended (TFX) pipeline to process and model clickstream data from multiple news sites. The pipeline includes several preprocessing steps, such as sessionization and normalization, before model training. Once in production, the pipeline will handle over 80 TB of BigQuery data each day. The team needs to run preprocessing at scale, store artifacts for reproducibility, and log experiment metrics to Vertex AI Experiments while keeping orchestration fully managed.

What should you do?

Choose only ONE best answer.

options.A: Use the TFX pipeline in Vertex AI Pipelines and configure preprocessing to run in a Vertex AI Training custom job
options.B: Run the TFX pipeline on Dataproc using Apache Beam for preprocessing and export metrics to Vertex AI Experiments manually
options.C: Configure the TFX pipeline to run in Vertex AI Pipelines and set Apache Beam parameters so preprocessing steps are executed in Dataflow
options.D: Run the TFX pipeline in Dataflow directly and configure Apache Beam to write logs to Vertex AI Experiments

explanation:
The correct approach is to run the TFX pipeline in Vertex AI Pipelines and set Apache Beam parameters so the preprocessing steps run in Dataflow. This leverages Vertex AI Pipelines for managed orchestration while ensuring large-scale distributed data processing is handled by Dataflow, which integrates cleanly with Apache Beam. It also enables direct tracking of artifacts and parameters in Vertex ML Metadata and logging of metrics in Vertex AI Experiments without adding orchestration complexity. This setup is well suited for processing tens of terabytes daily with high scalability.

The other options either remove the benefits of Vertex AI Pipelines or misuse services. Running Dataflow directly bypasses managed orchestration and reduces integration with Vertex AI metadata tracking. Using a Vertex AI Training custom job for preprocessing is inefficient since it is not designed for Apache Beam-based ETL workloads. Running on Dataproc with manual metric exports adds operational overhead and misses the native integration features between Vertex AI Pipelines and its metadata and experiment tracking systems.

question:
A law firm stores client deposition recordings in Cloud Storage for later review and analysis. The depositions are recorded using VoIP equipment at a 12 kHz sample rate. Most recordings last between 4 and 8 minutes. The legal team needs a transcription process that can handle these files reliably so they can be searched and referenced in case preparation. You need to follow Google Cloud’s best practices when setting up the transcription workflow.

What should you do?

Choose only ONE best answer.

options.A: Upsample the recordings to 24 kHz and use the Speech-to-Text API with synchronous transcription requests
options.B: Upsample the recordings to 24 kHz and use the Speech-to-Text API with long-running transcription requests
options.C: Keep the original 12 kHz recordings and use the Speech-to-Text API with asynchronous transcription requests
options.D: Keep the original 12 kHz recordings and use the Speech-to-Text API with synchronous transcription requests

explanation:
The correct approach is to keep the original 12 kHz recordings and use asynchronous transcription. The Speech-to-Text API can process 12 kHz audio directly, so there is no need to upsample. Since the recordings are several minutes long, asynchronous transcription is the appropriate method, as it can handle longer files without running into the time limits or reliability issues that synchronous transcription can face. This setup ensures the depositions can be transcribed accurately without unnecessary audio processing.

The other choices have clear drawbacks. Upsampling to 24 kHz adds extra processing time without improving transcription quality and can introduce distortion. Synchronous transcription on multi-minute recordings risks timeouts or partial transcripts. Combining upsampling with synchronous transcription not only adds unnecessary processing but also uses an unsuitable transcription method for the length of the recordings, making it the least effective choice.

question:
A financial analytics startup built a custom training script in PyTorch that can train either a decision tree regressor or a gradient boosting model, controlled by a model_type parameter. When model_type is set to gradient_boosting, additional parameters such as max_depth and learning_rate are used. You plan to use Vertex AI hyperparameter tuning with a trial budget of 120 runs. Your goal is to find the combination of model_type and associated hyperparameters that results in the best predictive accuracy.

What should you do?

Choose only ONE best answer.

options.A: Launch separate hyperparameter tuning jobs for each model_type, splitting the budget evenly, then compare their final results
options.B: Launch a single hyperparameter tuning job for 120 trials, making max_depth conditional on model_type but leaving learning_rate as a non-conditional hyperparameter
options.C: Launch a single hyperparameter tuning job for 120 trials, setting max_depth and learning_rate as conditional hyperparameters that only apply when model_type is gradient_boosting
options.D: Launch a single hyperparameter tuning job with model_type as the only hyperparameter, select the better performing model, and then run a second tuning job on its relevant hyperparameters

explanation:
The correct approach is to run one hyperparameter tuning job and make max_depth and learning_rate conditional on the parent hyperparameter model_type. This ensures that these two parameters are only considered when the gradient boosting model is being tested. By structuring the search space this way, Vertex AI focuses trials on valid combinations and avoids wasting resources testing irrelevant hyperparameters for the decision tree model. This method also allows both model types to be evaluated under the same tuning process, giving the optimizer the flexibility to find the best architecture and parameter settings within the same budget.

The other options create inefficiencies. Running separate jobs splits the budget, which limits exploration for each architecture. Running an initial job on model_type alone and then tuning only the selected one risks missing better combinations that could have emerged if tuning was done jointly. Making max_depth conditional but leaving learning_rate non-conditional wastes trials because learning_rate would still be evaluated for decision tree runs where it has no effect.

question:
You are training a TensorFlow model to classify satellite images stored in Cloud Storage. The dataset is too large to load into memory, and you need to build a scalable input pipeline that follows Google-recommended practices. What should you do?

Choose only ONE best answer.

options.A: Use tf.data.Dataset.prefetch() to load the dataset into memory before training
options.B: Convert the images to TFRecord format, store them in Cloud Storage, and read them with the tf.data API
options.C: Write a generator function that loads images on the fly using tf.io.read_file()
options.D: Import the image files directly without writing a pipeline

explanation: The recommended approach for large image datasets is to convert the data into TFRecords and use the tf.data API to read and preprocess the data. TFRecords provide an efficient binary format for large-scale datasets and allow data to be streamed from storage in batches, reducing memory pressure. The tf.data API supports operations like parallel reading and prefetching, but prefetch is only useful after the data is already in the pipeline. It cannot address the challenge of data that doesn’t fit in memory. Generators and raw reads lack the efficiency and scalability of tf.data pipelines. Importing the data directly without an input pipeline would not solve the problem because that's what's creating the problem in the first place.

question:
A subscription-based streaming service is analyzing user watch behavior stored in BigQuery to identify different viewer types. The team does not have labeled data and is unsure how many user groups exist. They want to find meaningful clusters of users based on patterns like viewing frequency, genre preferences, and session length. What is the most efficient approach to explore these patterns?

Choose only ONE best answer.

options.A: Export the data from BigQuery and use a Jupyter notebook to build a clustering model using scikit-learn
options.B: Create viewer categories manually in a spreadsheet and assign users to them using a lookup join in BigQuery
options.C: Use Looker Studio to create visual dashboards that highlight usage trends for individual users
options.D: Build a k-means clustering model with BigQuery ML and allow the model to estimate the number of clusters

explanation: The best option is to use BigQuery ML to perform k-means clustering directly on the watch behavior data. This allows the team to apply unsupervised learning using SQL without requiring labeled data or leaving the BigQuery environment. The model can automatically select the number of clusters using internal evaluation techniques. Building a model in a notebook requires exporting data and managing infrastructure, which adds unnecessary overhead. Manually defining categories imposes assumptions and removes the ability to discover emergent user groups. Dashboards are helpful for monitoring, but they do not perform clustering or pattern discovery. BigQuery ML provides the most efficient path to uncovering underlying behavior segments without needing prior labels or external tooling.

question:
You are developing an object detection model with TensorFlow and training it on Vertex AI using four GPUs and tf.distribute.MirroredStrategy. You previously trained the model on a single GPU and saw much faster iteration time than with your multi-GPU setup. You made no other changes between runs. What change should you try first to reduce training time?

Choose only ONE best answer.

options.A: Enable prefetching with tf.data.AUTOTUNE
options.B: Switch to a TPU and use tf.distribute.TPUStrategy
options.C: Increase the global batch size
options.D: Use mixed precision training with bfloat16

explanation:
When using MirroredStrategy to distribute training across multiple GPUs, keeping the same batch size as in single-GPU training causes each GPU to process only a small portion of data, which leads to poor resource utilization. Increasing the batch size helps each GPU handle a full workload in parallel, making the distribution more efficient.

Enabling AUTOTUNE improves data pipeline performance but does not address the core scaling issue here. Switching to TPUs may eventually improve performance but requires changes to the environment and setup. Mixed precision training can help with memory and speed, but if the batch size is still too small, it won't resolve the underutilization problem across multiple GPUs.
